Genotype likelihoods, priors, and their use in an ancestry HMM

  • Show All Code
  • Hide All Code
  • View Source

Single-read calling under low coverage — where the prior lives, and what accumulation can and cannot do

Author

Fausto Rodríguez-Zapata

Published

July 16, 2026

At ~0.4× coverage the SNP50K genotype matrix looks pathological: 70% of cells are missing, of the rest 97% are hom-REF, essentially none are hom-ALT, and wherever a minority (ALT) read appears the site is overwhelmingly called heterozygous (the depth-split below). Taken at face value that reads as a genome that is almost entirely B73 with a thin, ubiquitous sprinkle of heterozygosity — which is not what a BC2S3 NIL is. This note asks the two questions that pattern forces:

  1. What produced it — the reads, or the caller? Why does a single ALT read become a HET call; when is that the right call, and when is it just the genotype prior asserting itself?
  2. Can per-site genotype confidence be recovered downstream? Does accumulating genotype likelihoods (GLs) across a gene / haplotype stretch / fixed bin rescue the calls, or is the information simply not there?
NoteMotivation — the pattern this note explains

The SNP50K cohort (1,439 skim NILs, ~0.4×) was genotyped with a posterior / HWE caller (bcftools call -mv). Across all 1,439 × 50,995 = 73,381,805 genotype cells:

class count % of all % of called
REF-hom 0/0 21,168,920 28.85 97.02
HET 648,539 0.88 2.97
ALT-hom 1,001 0.00 0.00
missing ./. 51,563,345 70.27 —

Splitting the same cells by read depth shows why — at a single read, 448,805 cells are called HET but only 355 hom-ALT, against 15.9 M REF:

depth REF HET ALT missing
0 40,524 1 0 51,563,345
1 15,868,677 448,805 355 0
2 4,057,642 154,596 138 0
3–5 1,184,345 44,361 343 0
6–10 17,559 727 163 0
>10 173 49 2 0

A lone ALT read is ~1,000× more likely to be called HET than hom-ALT — the opposite of the single-read likelihood, which favors hom-ALT 2:1 (§1). Why the prior overturns the read, and why that is a caller choice (bcftools/GLIMPSE vs GATK), is the subject of §2. Full counts + provenance: snp50k-cohort-provenance.

Short answer. (1) The pattern is the caller’s prior, not biology: a MAP / HWE caller (bcftools, GLIMPSE, …) turns any lone minority read into a HET because the rare-allele prior overturns the 2:1 single-read likelihood for hom-ALT; GATK, being maximum-likelihood, instead scatters spurious hom-ALT. Neither is trustworthy per site at this depth. (2) GLs are the correct currency, and accumulation across a stretch does help — but only for a shared latent state (the ancestry of the segment), which the count-based HMM already exploits because the read counts are the GL’s sufficient statistic. Accumulation does not manufacture per-site zygosity information, and the dilution ceiling (d ≈ 0.3, snp50k-genotype-identifiability.md) is unchanged by moving from counts to GLs.

Notation: biallelic site, reference (B73) allele \(A\), alternate (donor) allele \(B\); genotypes \(\{AA, AB, BB\}=\{0/0, 0/1, 1/1\}\); per-base error \(\varepsilon = 10^{-Q/10}\).

1. The single-site genotype likelihood — identical core in GATK, ANGSD, samtools

All three tools compute the likelihood \(P(\text{reads}\mid G)\) per genotype with the same diploid-averaging model (ANGSD calls it “the GATK/dragon model”; samtools adds a read-dependency term that vanishes at depth 1):

\[ P(\text{reads}\mid G=\{A_1,A_2\}) = \prod_{i}\Big[\tfrac12 P(b_i\mid A_1) + \tfrac12 P(b_i\mid A_2)\Big], \qquad P(b\mid A) = \begin{cases} 1-\varepsilon & b = A\\[2pt] \varepsilon/3 & b \neq A. \end{cases} \]

Each allele of the diploid genotype contributes with weight \(\tfrac12\) — this is why the heterozygote likelihood of any single read is pinned near \(\tfrac12\).

One ALT read (\(b=B\), \(\varepsilon=0.01\))

genotype likelihood \(P(\text{read}\mid G)\) value Phred \(-10\log_{10}L\) normalized PL
\(AA\) \(\varepsilon/3\) 0.0033 24.8 25
\(AB\) \(\tfrac12\!\cdot\!\tfrac{\varepsilon}{3} + \tfrac12(1-\varepsilon)\) 0.498 3.0 3
\(BB\) \(1-\varepsilon\) 0.99 0.04 0
  • PL \(= -10\log_{10} L\), normalized by subtracting the smallest so the best genotype is 0 (the GATK worked example: probabilities \(100\times\) apart give PLs \(20\) apart). PL is a likelihood, emitted under an effectively flat genotype prior — despite GATK’s docs writing “\(P(\text{Genotype}\mid\text{Data})\)”.
  • GQ \(=\) (2nd-smallest PL) \(-\) (smallest PL), capped at 99. Here GQ = 3.

So by likelihood alone, one alt read points to BB (homozygous-alt), GQ 3 — a \(\approx 2{:}1\) call (\(\tfrac{1-\varepsilon}{1/2}\approx 2 \Rightarrow 3\) PL). That 3-PL likelihood ratio is the entire information content of one read. No downstream step adds to it; it can only be combined with a prior or with other sites.

2. Likelihood → posterior: where the prior lives, and what swapping it does

The genotype call and its meaning come from the posterior

\[ P(G \mid \text{reads}) \;\propto\; \underbrace{P(\text{reads}\mid G)}_{\text{GL — identical across tools}} \;\times\; \underbrace{\pi(G)}_{\text{prior — tool-specific, swappable}} . \]

Where each tool applies \(\pi\):

tool GL stage prior stage
GATK HaplotypeCaller PairHMM → PL (likelihood) GenotypeGVCFs allele-freq/HWE prior (--heterozygosity, default \(10^{-3}\)); CalculateGenotypePosteriors for custom (pedigree / population-AF) priors
ANGSD -GL models (GATK / samtools / SOAPsnp) → GLs, no calling by default -doPost 1 = HWE prior at estimated allele freq; -doPost 2 = uniform
bcftools mpileup (Li 2011) → GLs call -m allele-freq prior with -P mutation-rate hyperprior

HWE prior at the cohort frequency → forces HET

The genotype prior is Hardy–Weinberg at the cohort alt-allele frequency \(p\) (the GL-based cohort mean \(\bar p = 0.015\); provenance in §2.2): \(\pi(AA,AB,BB)=((1-p)^2,\,2p(1-p),\,p^2)\). Multiplying the one-read likelihoods (from §1) by this prior and normalizing, at \(p=\bar p=0.015\) and \(\varepsilon=0.01\):

genotype likelihood \(P(\text{read}\mid G)\) \(L\) HWE prior \(\pi(G)\) \(\pi\) posterior \(\propto L\cdot\pi\) \(P(G\mid\text{read})\)
\(AA\) \(\varepsilon/3\) 0.0033 \((1-p)^2\) 0.970 \(\tfrac{\varepsilon}{3}\cdot(1-p)^2\) 0.178
\(AB\) \(\tfrac12\!\cdot\!\tfrac{\varepsilon}{3} + \tfrac12(1-\varepsilon)\) 0.498 \(2p(1-p)\) 0.0296 \(p(1-p)\) 0.810
\(BB\) \(1-\varepsilon\) 0.99 \(p^2\) 0.000225 \((1-\varepsilon)\cdot p^2\) 0.012

Taking the ratio of the two non-REF rows’ posteriors (\(\propto L\cdot\pi\)), HET beats ALT-hom by

\[ \frac{P(AB\mid\text{read})}{P(BB\mid\text{read})} = \frac{p(1-p)}{(1-\varepsilon)\cdot p^2} = \frac{1-p}{(1-\varepsilon)\cdot p} \approx \frac1p \approx 66 , \]

matching the table’s \(0.810/0.012 \approx 67\). The rare-allele prior overturns the \(2{:}1\) likelihood — it collapses the ALT-hom posterior to ~1% and hands 81% to the heterozygote, even though the read’s likelihood most favored \(BB\). The call is the prior’s, not the read’s. This is exactly the depth-1 row of the motivating table above — 448,805 HET vs 355 ALT calls on single reads — and the overall 97% REF / 0.00% ALT-hom pattern in the SNP50K matrix (snp50k-cohort-provenance.md).

Posterior callers inherit this, and most assume HWE. bcftools call -m, freebayes, ANGSD -doPost, and the imputation tools Beagle and GLIMPSE are all posterior callers: each weights the genotype likelihood by a prior and reports the genotype that maximizes the product, the maximum-a-posteriori (MAP) estimate.

\[ \hat G = \arg\max_G P(G\mid\text{data}) = \arg\max_G P(\text{data}\mid G)\,\pi(G) . \]

The maximum-likelihood choice, by contrast, keeps only the likelihood \(P(\text{data}\mid G)\) and drops the prior \(\pi\). The prior these callers assume is almost always Hardy–Weinberg: at the estimated allele frequency for bcftools, freebayes, and ANGSD -doPost, and at a reference panel’s frequencies for Beagle and GLIMPSE. So at a rare site every one of them turns a lone ALT read into a HET call. The HWE prior, not the read, decides.

GATK is the exception. It does not call genotypes on the posterior: its per-sample GT is the maximum-likelihood genotype, and the heterozygosity prior only gates whether the site is variant. There a lone ALT read is called hom-ALT, not HET. The companion note GATK does not call genotypes on posteriors explains why, and why GATK’s own heterozygote excess has a separate paralog and coverage cause.

Breeding prior — the single-locus NIL genotype frequencies

The NIL design is not HWE. Selfing after backcross drives heterozygotes to homozygotes. Single-locus genotype frequencies (donor allele freq \(0.125\); selfing preserves it):

design \(f_{AA}\) (REF-hom) \(f_{AB}\) (het) \(f_{BB}\) (donor-hom) het : donor-hom | introgressed
BC2S2 (bulked skim) 0.844 0.0625 0.0938 40 : 60
BC2S3 (nominal NIL) 0.859 0.0312 0.1094 22 : 78

Note the sign — \(f_{BB} > f_{AB}\) — by design, an introgressed locus is more often donor-homozygous than heterozygous (78% vs 22% for BC2S3), because selfing removes heterozygosity. (This corrects a claim in an earlier draft that the introgression is “mostly het” — the design expects mostly donor-hom; the callers report mostly het only because donor-hom is undetectable at \(0.4\times\), §5.)

Swapping the prior flips the single-read call

The same one alt read, now with the BC2S3 breeding prior (likelihood from §1 \(\times\) prior, at \(\varepsilon=0.01\), posterior normalized to sum to 1):

genotype likelihood \(P(\text{read}\mid G)\) \(L\) BC2S3 prior \(\pi(G)\) \(\pi\) posterior \(\propto L\cdot\pi\) \(P(G\mid\text{read})\)
\(AA\) \(\varepsilon/3\) 0.0033 \(f_{AA}\) 0.859 \(\tfrac{\varepsilon}{3}\cdot f_{AA}\) 0.023
\(AB\) \(\tfrac12\!\cdot\!\tfrac{\varepsilon}{3} + \tfrac12(1-\varepsilon)\) 0.498 \(f_{AB}\) 0.0312 \(\tfrac12 f_{AB}\) 0.123
\(BB\) \(1-\varepsilon\) 0.99 \(f_{BB}\) 0.1094 \((1-\varepsilon)\cdot f_{BB}\) 0.855

Taking the ratio of the two non-REF rows’ posteriors, HET now loses to ALT-hom:

\[ \frac{P(AB\mid\text{read})}{P(BB\mid\text{read})} = \frac{\tfrac12 f_{AB}}{(1-\varepsilon)\cdot f_{BB}} = \frac{0.5 \times 0.0312}{0.99 \times 0.1094} \approx 0.14 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \textbf{DONOR-HOM} \ (\approx 7{:}1), \]

matching the table’s \(0.123/0.855\).

So the identical read and likelihood is called HET under the HWE prior and DONOR-HOM under the breeding prior. This is the crux for the “fix the prior” argument: swapping HWE → breeding frequencies is correct and worthwhile (it counteracts the coverage-induced het-bias), but it only relabels the default — it adds no zygosity information. The data still speaks at only \(\approx 3\) PL. One read cannot resolve het from hom under any prior; the prior decides the tie.

The four priors side by side — and where each allele frequency comes from

We have now seen the two extremes — the HWE-at-cohort prior forces HET, the BC2S3 breeding prior forces DONOR-HOM. Putting all four priors a real pipeline might actually use into one comparison makes the crux explicit: two of them are HWE priors, and their allele frequencies have completely different origins.

# prior (caller) \(\pi(AA, AB, BB)\) allele-frequency source
1 ML flat — GATK single-sample \((\tfrac13,\tfrac13,\tfrac13)\) none; per-sample GT \(=\arg\max\) PL
2 GATK joint — GenotypeGVCFs HWE at \(\hat p = 0.125\) theoretical BC2S3 donor freq, \(f_{BB}+f_{AB}/2\)
3 bcftools — mpileup \| call -m HWE at \(p = 0.015\) empirical, GL-based cohort mean
4 BC2S3 breeding design \((0.859, 0.031, 0.109)\) single-locus Mendelian NIL frequencies

GATK sits in two rows: its per-sample GT is flat/ML (bar 1), but its joint step (GenotypeGVCFs, and CalculateGenotypePosteriors) applies HWE at the estimated cohort frequency (bar 2) — so joint-genotyped GATK is not exempt from the het-forcing prior.

2.1 The theoretical frequency \(\hat p = 0.125\) (GATK joint)

Bar 2 uses the frequency implied by the BC2S3 design itself, not measured from data: \(\hat p = f_{BB} + f_{AB}/2 = 0.1094 + 0.0312/2 = 0.125\). This isolates the effect of the HWE shape: even when a joint caller estimates the frequency perfectly, HWE at \(\hat p = 0.125\) gives \(\pi = (0.766, 0.219, 0.016)\) — donor-hom is \(\hat p^2 = 0.016\), about \(14\times\) rarer than het (\(2\hat p(1-\hat p)=0.219\)). So the joint call on one read is HET at 7:1, despite the frequency being right. The joint bar is the theoretical BC2S3 expectation run through HWE.

2.2 The empirical frequency \(p = 0.015\) — GL-based, not a hard-call count

Bar 3 uses the measured genome-wide mean alt-allele frequency of the real cohort, \(\bar p = 0.015\) — far below the per-locus \(0.125\) because most panel sites are near-monomorphic REF across the cohort. There are two ways to obtain such a number, and they are not equivalent:

  • From genotype likelihoods (correct at low coverage). Estimate \(p\) directly from the per-sample GLs by EM (Li 2011; ANGSD -doMaf/-doPost 1; bcftools call -m), marginalizing over the unknown genotypes — no genotype is ever hard-called. Each sample contributes its expected alt dosage \(0\cdot P(AA)+1\cdot P(AB)+2\cdot P(BB)\).
  • From called genotypes (circular). Counting REF/ALT alleles in the called matrix instead re-uses calls that were themselves made under the het-forcing HWE prior: the prior inflates het, the het-inflated calls are counted to estimate \(p\), and \(p\) feeds the prior again.

\(\bar p = 0.015\) here is the GL-based estimate — the posterior-caller core (bcftools/ANGSD, Li 2011 → GLs → allele-freq prior) estimates \(p\) from likelihoods and then imposes HWE\((\bar p)\). The 448,805-vs-355 depth-1 table above is the consequence of that imposed prior, not the source of \(\bar p\).

If a local pipeline instead computed \(0.015\) by counting alleles in a hard-called VCF, that would change the reading from “correct estimator, wrong HWE shape” to “circular estimate and wrong shape.” (-doMaf = GL-based; bcftools query on a called VCF = hard-call.)

2.3 Why no single HWE frequency represents BC2S3 — and the one caller that can

BC2S3 has a het deficit: \(f_{BB}=0.109 > f_{AB}=0.031\) (donor-hom more common than het, because selfing collapses heterozygotes). No HWE frequency reproduces that — HWE forces \(f_{AB}=2p(1-p)\ge f_{BB}=p^2\) for all \(p\le\tfrac23\). So GATK’s joint step and CalculateGenotypePosteriors (both HWE-locked) are structurally unable to emit the BC2S3 call, whatever frequency they estimate.

The fix is an inbreeding term. The inbreeding-adjusted HWE prior at frequency \(p\) with coefficient \(F\) is \[ \pi = \big((1-p)^2+Fp(1-p),\;\; 2p(1-p)(1-F),\;\; p^2+Fp(1-p)\big), \] and at \(p=0.125,\ F=0.857\) this is exactly \((0.859, 0.031, 0.109)\) — the BC2S3 vector (\(F = 1 - f_{AB}/[2p(1-p)] = 0.857\)). Selfing is inbreeding, so the design’s single-locus frequencies are the \(F\)-model frequencies, not an ad-hoc table.

Which callers can apply it? GATK (GenotypeGVCFs, CalculateGenotypePosteriors), bcftools (call -m), freebayes, Beagle, and GLIMPSE are all HWE-at-\(p\) (i.e. \(F=0\)) or reference-panel priors, with no inbreeding knob — they cannot encode \(f_{BB}>f_{AB}\). ANGSD is the exception: with a per-individual inbreeding coefficient from ngsF / ngsF-HMM / PCAngsd (Vieira et al. 2013, ref. 8), its -doPost computes the \(F\)-adjusted genotype posterior, reproducing the BC2S3 prior. (Caveat: \(F\) is a genome-wide per-individual coefficient, not a per-locus segregating-introgression prior, and \(p\) is still GL-estimated — but for the het-deficient shape it is exact.) So the breeding prior is the \(F\)-model prior, and ANGSD -doPost +F is its standard implementation.

The four priors on one ALT read

Posterior \(\propto\) likelihood \(\times\) prior, normalized. Same read, same likelihood (PL \(=[25,3,0]\)); only the prior changes:

prior (caller) \(\pi(AA, AB, BB)\) \(P(AA)\) \(P(AB)\) \(P(BB)\) call AB : BB
ML flat (GATK single-sample) \((0.333, 0.333, 0.333)\) 0.002 0.333 0.664 donor-hom 0.5
GATK joint, HWE \(\hat p=0.125\) \((0.766, 0.219, 0.016)\) 0.020 0.858 0.122 het 7.0
bcftools, HWE \(p=0.015\) \((0.970, 0.030, 0.0002)\) 0.178 0.809 0.012 het 65.9
BC2S3 breeding \((0.859, 0.031, 0.109)\) 0.023 0.122 0.855 donor-hom 0.1

Reading. Flat (GATK single-sample) and BC2S3 call donor-hom on one ALT read; both HWE priors call het — GATK-joint (theoretical \(\hat p=0.125\)) at 7:1, bcftools (empirical \(p=0.015\)) at 66:1. The call is the prior’s, not the read’s.

And the number of ALT reads each prior needs before it will finally call donor-hom (with \(r=0\) REF reads):

prior (caller) min ALT reads to \(P(\text{donor-hom}) > 0.5\)
ML flat (GATK single-sample) 1
BC2S3 breeding 1
GATK joint (HWE \(\hat p=0.125\)) 4
bcftools (HWE \(p=0.015\)) 8
Show code 
source(here::here("scripts/fig_gl_prior_lowcov.R"))
fig

3. From one site to a stretch: what accumulation can and cannot do

Accumulating across a gene / haplotype / bin is the right instinct. The subtlety is what latent variable the accumulation is evidence for. Two targets:

(a) The genotype at each individual site. Borrowing across sites requires an explicit haplotype/LD model — a reference panel (Beagle/GLIMPSE, Li–Stephens) or a founder graph (PHG). Multiplying GLs across sites of different genotypes is meaningless without such a model to link them. This route needs the donor founders / phased panel we lack, and teosinte’s short-range LD undercuts it.

(b) A single latent state shared over the stretch — the ancestry (REF / HET / donor-hom segment, or “which founder”). If the stretch has one state \(S\), each site’s reads are a conditionally-independent emission and the product concentrates the posterior on \(S\):

\[ P(S \mid \text{reads}_{1:n}) \;\propto\; \pi(S)\prod_{j=1}^{n} \underbrace{\Big[\textstyle\sum_{G} P(\text{reads}_j\mid G)\,P(G\mid S)\Big]}_{\text{emission}_j \;=\; \sum (\text{GL}) \times (\text{state-conditional genotype dist})}. \]

This is exactly RTIGER / binhmm / PHG’s findPaths. It works without a panel because the linkage is physical contiguity plus the breeding design (few recombinations) — not population LD.

The count emission is the GL emission

For a biallelic site under an independent per-read error model, the pair \((n_{\text{ref}}, n_{\text{alt}})\) is the sufficient statistic of the per-site genotype likelihood: the emission \(\sum_G P(\text{reads}_j\mid G)P(G\mid S)\) depends on the reads only through those counts. nilHMM’s count / BetaBinomial emission is a direct parameterization of this (the BetaBinomial simply adds overdispersion for mapping/paralog noise). Therefore:

“Use genotype likelihoods in the HMM instead of counts” is, for a biallelic site, already implemented — phrased as counts. The caller never hard-calls per site (so it never inherits the bcftools 0%-ALT-hom prior artifact); it feeds soft per-site evidence into the along-chromosome HMM and decodes the segment.

What full GLs add over raw counts is real but marginal at 1 read/site: per-base quality weighting (down-weight low-\(Q\) bases rather than counting equally) and mapping-quality / multiallelic handling. The information axis is the same: \((n_{\text{ref}}, n_{\text{alt}})\) and their qualities.

4. Two different HMMs — do not conflate GATK’s PairHMM

GATK’s HaplotypeCaller does use an HMM, but it is orthogonal to the one Ruben wants:

GATK PairHMM ancestry HMM (RTIGER / nilHMM / PHG)
hidden path over bases within one read marker sites along the chromosome
emission read base vs candidate haplotype (indels, base-Q) reads at a site vs latent ancestry state
purpose local re-assembly / alignment likelihood \(P(\text{read}\mid\text{hap})\) segment ancestry / genotype imputation

GATK implements the alignment PairHMM, not the along-genome ancestry HMM. “They already do this” is true only for the alignment step; it is not a reusable component for ancestry segmentation. Keep the two distinct in the paper.

5. What survives — the information ceiling

Accumulation via the ancestry HMM buys a confident segment state, and the per-marker genotype is then that state projected/imputed onto each marker — PHG-style, and valid for GWAS/QTL (the mapping unit is the segment/dosage genotype). It is not an independent per-site zygosity measurement.

Three limits no GL/HMM refinement removes:

  1. The signal is compressed, not hidden by hard-calling. Outbred-donor dilution puts the states at alt-fractions \(\approx\{0,\,d/2,\,d\}=\{0,\,0.15,\,0.30\}\) with \(d\approx0.3\) (empirically confirmed, snp50k-genotype-identifiability.md §2.5). GLs cannot create information the reads do not carry.
  2. Het vs donor-hom stays weak — \(0.15\) vs \(0.30\) separation. A breeding prior (\(f_{BB}>f_{AB}\)) helps counteract the coverage het-bias, but does not resolve the zygosity.
  3. The real levers are upstream, not in the emission: (i) site selection to donor-informative markers (raise \(d\) toward 1, restoring \(\{0,\tfrac12,1\}\) separation), (ii) fit the emission means per taxon, (iii) use the BC2S2/BC2S3 prior in place of HWE.

Bottom line for the “GLs will rescue it” claim: GLs are the right soft evidence and the ancestry HMM is the right accumulator — and both are essentially what the count-emission caller already does. They rescue the segment/haplotype call (and the imputed marker genotypes), not the isolated site, and they do not lift the dilution ceiling.

NoteCorrections to earlier drafts of this analysis
# superseded statement correction
C1 “GATK/samtools apply a default HWE-ish genotype prior expecting 25–50% hets.” GATK single-sample GT is flat-prior \(\arg\max\) PL — no genotype prior. HWE enters only in the joint step (GenotypeGVCFs / CalculateGenotypePosteriors), as HWE at the estimated cohort \(p\) (§2, §2.1).
C2 Het overcalling framed as a multi-read \((\varepsilon/3)^r\) likelihood wall at 4×/12×. The governing regime is single-read at 0.4× (81% of covered sites have exactly one read); the overcalling is the rare-allele HWE prior, not a likelihood wall (§2).
C3 “RAF < 0.5 means reference over-counted.” Backwards. RAF \(=\) REF/(REF+ALT); reference-mapping bias over-represents REF \(\Rightarrow\) RAF \(>0.5\). A clean pipeline centers RAF at 0.5.
C4 “The EM under-estimates the allele frequency because of the het deficit.” It over-estimates (\(\hat p \approx 0.139\) vs true \(0.125\)): the donor-hom excess pulls \(p\) up under HWE. The frequency is ~right; the HWE shape is wrong (§2.3).
C5 “The introgressed locus is mostly het.” BC2S3 is het-deficient: \(f_{BB}\) (0.109) \(> f_{AB}\) (0.031). By design an introgressed locus is more often donor-hom than het (§2.3).

References

Genotype-likelihood models and callers

  1. Li H (2011). A statistical framework for SNP calling, mutation discovery, association mapping and population genetical parameter estimation from sequencing data. Bioinformatics 27(21):2987–2993. doi:10.1093/bioinformatics/btr509. — the samtools/bcftools GL model.
  2. Korneliussen TS, Albrechtsen A, Nielsen R (2014). ANGSD: Analysis of Next Generation Sequencing Data. BMC Bioinformatics 15:356. doi:10.1186/s12859-014-0356-4. — GL-model taxonomy + likelihood-propagation framework; software page: https://www.popgen.dk/angsd/index.php/Genotype_Likelihoods.
  3. Poplin R, Ruano-Rubio V, DePristo MA, et al. (2017). Scaling accurate genetic variant discovery to tens of thousands of samples. bioRxiv 201178. doi:10.1101/201178. — GATK HaplotypeCaller.
  4. DePristo MA, Banks E, Poplin R, et al. (2011). A framework for variation discovery and genotyping using next-generation DNA sequencing data. Nature Genetics 43(5):491–498. doi:10.1038/ng.806. — the GATK framework; PL/GQ mechanics documented at https://gatk.broadinstitute.org/hc/en-us/articles/360035890451.

Linkage / imputation models

  1. Li N, Stephens M (2003). Modeling linkage disequilibrium and identifying recombination hotspots using single-nucleotide polymorphism data. Genetics 165(4):2213–2233. doi:10.1093/genetics/165.4.2213. — the haplotype-mosaic (Li–Stephens) model behind panel imputation (§3a).
  2. Jensen SE, Charles JR, Muleta K, et al. (2020). A sorghum practical haplotype graph facilitates genome-wide imputation and cost-effective genomic prediction. The Plant Genome 13(1):e20009. doi:10.1002/tpg2.20009. — the founder-graph (PHG) route (§3, §5).
  3. Hui R, D’Atanasio E, Cassidy LM, Scheib CL, Kivisild T (2020). Evaluating genotype imputation pipeline for ultra-low coverage ancient genomes. Scientific Reports 10:18542. doi:10.1038/s41598-020-75387-w. — reference-panel imputation at ultra-low coverage (§3a).

Inbreeding-aware genotype calling

  1. Vieira FG, Fumagalli M, Albrechtsen A, Nielsen R (2013). Estimating inbreeding coefficients from NGS data: impact on genotype calling and allele frequency estimation. Genome Research 23(11):1852–1861. doi:10.1101/gr.157388.113. — the per-individual \(F\)-adjusted genotype posterior (ANGSD ngsF, §2.3).

Companion to snp50k-genotype-identifiability.md and snp50k-cohort-provenance.md. 2026-07-02.

Source Code
---
title: "Genotype likelihoods, priors, and their use in an ancestry HMM"
subtitle: "Single-read calling under low coverage — where the prior lives, and what accumulation can and cannot do"
author: "Fausto Rodríguez-Zapata"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    embed-resources: true
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
  echo: true
---

At ~0.4× coverage the SNP50K genotype matrix looks pathological: 70% of cells
are missing, of the rest **97% are hom-REF**, essentially **none are hom-ALT**,
and wherever a minority (ALT) read appears the site is overwhelmingly called
**heterozygous** (the depth-split below). Taken at face value that reads as a
genome that is almost entirely B73 with a thin, ubiquitous sprinkle of
heterozygosity — which is *not* what a BC2S3 NIL is. This note asks the two
questions that pattern forces:

1. **What produced it — the reads, or the caller?** Why does a single ALT read
   become a HET call; when is that the right call, and when is it just the
   genotype prior asserting itself?
2. **Can per-site genotype confidence be recovered downstream?** Does
   accumulating genotype likelihoods (GLs) across a gene / haplotype stretch /
   fixed bin rescue the calls, or is the information simply not there?

::: {.callout-note title="Motivation — the pattern this note explains"}
The SNP50K cohort (1,439 skim NILs, ~0.4×) was genotyped with a posterior / HWE
caller (`bcftools call -mv`). Across all 1,439 × 50,995 = 73,381,805 genotype
cells:

| class          | count       | % of all | % of called |
|----------------|------------:|---------:|------------:|
| REF-hom `0/0`  | 21,168,920  |    28.85 |       97.02 |
| HET            |    648,539  |     0.88 |        2.97 |
| ALT-hom        |      1,001  |     0.00 |        0.00 |
| missing `./.`  | 51,563,345  |    70.27 |        —    |

Splitting the same cells by read depth shows *why* — **at a single read, 448,805
cells are called HET but only 355 hom-ALT**, against 15.9 M REF:

| depth | REF        | HET     | ALT | missing     |
|-------|-----------:|--------:|----:|------------:|
| 0     |     40,524 |       1 |   0 | 51,563,345  |
| 1     | 15,868,677 | 448,805 | 355 |           0 |
| 2     |  4,057,642 | 154,596 | 138 |           0 |
| 3–5   |  1,184,345 |  44,361 | 343 |           0 |
| 6–10  |     17,559 |     727 | 163 |           0 |
| >10   |        173 |      49 |   2 |           0 |

A lone ALT read is ~1,000× more likely to be called **HET** than hom-**ALT** —
the *opposite* of the single-read likelihood, which favors hom-ALT 2:1 (§1).
Why the prior overturns the read, and why that is a **caller choice**
(bcftools/GLIMPSE vs GATK), is the subject of §2. Full counts + provenance:
[snp50k-cohort-provenance](https://github.com/sawers-rellan-labs/zealtiger/blob/main/docs/snp50k-cohort-provenance.md).
:::

**Short answer.** *(1)* The pattern is the **caller's prior, not biology**: a
MAP / HWE caller (`bcftools`, GLIMPSE, …) turns any lone minority read into a HET
because the rare-allele prior overturns the 2:1 single-read likelihood for
hom-ALT; GATK, being maximum-likelihood, instead scatters spurious hom-ALT.
Neither is trustworthy per site at this depth. *(2)* GLs are the correct
currency, and accumulation across a stretch *does* help — but only for a
**shared latent state** (the ancestry of the segment), which the count-based HMM
already exploits because the read counts *are* the GL's sufficient statistic.
Accumulation does **not** manufacture per-site zygosity information, and the
dilution ceiling (`d ≈ 0.3`,
[snp50k-genotype-identifiability.md](snp50k_genotype_identifiability.html)) is
unchanged by moving from counts to GLs.

Notation: biallelic site, reference (B73) allele $A$, alternate (donor) allele $B$;
genotypes $\{AA, AB, BB\}=\{0/0, 0/1, 1/1\}$; per-base error
$\varepsilon = 10^{-Q/10}$.

------

## 1. The single-site genotype likelihood — identical core in GATK, ANGSD, samtools

All three tools compute the **likelihood** $P(\text{reads}\mid G)$ per genotype with
the same diploid-averaging model (ANGSD calls it "the GATK/dragon model"; samtools
adds a read-dependency term that vanishes at depth 1):

$$
P(\text{reads}\mid G=\{A_1,A_2\}) = \prod_{i}\Big[\tfrac12 P(b_i\mid A_1) + \tfrac12 P(b_i\mid A_2)\Big],
\qquad
P(b\mid A) = \begin{cases} 1-\varepsilon & b = A\\[2pt] \varepsilon/3 & b \neq A. \end{cases}
$$

Each allele of the diploid genotype contributes with weight $\tfrac12$ — this is
why the heterozygote likelihood of any single read is pinned near $\tfrac12$.

### One ALT read ($b=B$, $\varepsilon=0.01$)

| genotype | likelihood $P(\text{read}\mid G)$ | value | Phred $-10\log_{10}L$ | normalized PL |
|---|---|---:|---:|---:|
| $AA$ | $\varepsilon/3$ | 0.0033 | 24.8 | **25** |
| $AB$ | $\tfrac12\!\cdot\!\tfrac{\varepsilon}{3} + \tfrac12(1-\varepsilon)$ | 0.498 | 3.0 | **3** |
| $BB$ | $1-\varepsilon$ | 0.99 | 0.04 | **0** |

- **PL** $= -10\log_{10} L$, normalized by subtracting the smallest so the best
  genotype is 0 (the GATK worked example: probabilities $100\times$ apart give PLs
  $20$ apart). PL is a *likelihood*, emitted under an effectively flat genotype
  prior — despite GATK's docs writing "$P(\text{Genotype}\mid\text{Data})$".
- **GQ** $=$ (2nd-smallest PL) $-$ (smallest PL), capped at 99. Here **GQ = 3**.

So *by likelihood alone*, one alt read points to **BB (homozygous-alt), GQ 3** —
a $\approx 2{:}1$ call ($\tfrac{1-\varepsilon}{1/2}\approx 2 \Rightarrow 3$ PL).
**That 3-PL likelihood ratio is the entire information content of one read.** No
downstream step adds to it; it can only be combined with a prior or with *other
sites*.

------

## 2. Likelihood → posterior: where the prior lives, and what swapping it does

The genotype **call** and its meaning come from the posterior

$$
P(G \mid \text{reads}) \;\propto\; \underbrace{P(\text{reads}\mid G)}_{\text{GL — identical across tools}} \;\times\; \underbrace{\pi(G)}_{\text{prior — tool-specific, swappable}} .
$$

Where each tool applies $\pi$:

| tool | GL stage | prior stage |
|---|---|---|
| **GATK** | `HaplotypeCaller` PairHMM → PL (likelihood) | `GenotypeGVCFs` allele-freq/HWE prior (`--heterozygosity`, default $10^{-3}$); `CalculateGenotypePosteriors` for **custom** (pedigree / population-AF) priors |
| **ANGSD** | `-GL` models (GATK / samtools / SOAPsnp) → GLs, *no calling by default* | `-doPost 1` = HWE prior at estimated allele freq; `-doPost 2` = uniform |
| **bcftools** | `mpileup` (Li 2011) → GLs | `call -m` allele-freq prior with `-P` mutation-rate hyperprior |

### HWE prior at the cohort frequency → forces HET

The genotype prior is Hardy–Weinberg at the cohort alt-allele frequency $p$
(the GL-based cohort mean $\bar p = 0.015$; provenance in §2.2):
$\pi(AA,AB,BB)=((1-p)^2,\,2p(1-p),\,p^2)$.
Multiplying the one-read likelihoods (from §1) by this prior and normalizing, at
$p=\bar p=0.015$ and $\varepsilon=0.01$:

| genotype | likelihood $P(\text{read}\mid G)$ | $L$ | HWE prior $\pi(G)$ | $\pi$ | posterior $\propto L\cdot\pi$ | $P(G\mid\text{read})$ |
|---|---|---:|---|---:|---|---:|
| $AA$ | $\varepsilon/3$ | 0.0033 | $(1-p)^2$ | 0.970 | $\tfrac{\varepsilon}{3}\cdot(1-p)^2$ | 0.178 |
| $AB$ | $\tfrac12\!\cdot\!\tfrac{\varepsilon}{3} + \tfrac12(1-\varepsilon)$ | 0.498 | $2p(1-p)$ | 0.0296 | $p(1-p)$ | **0.810** |
| $BB$ | $1-\varepsilon$ | 0.99 | $p^2$ | 0.000225 | $(1-\varepsilon)\cdot p^2$ | 0.012 |

Taking the ratio of the two non-REF rows' posteriors ($\propto L\cdot\pi$), HET
beats ALT-hom by

$$
\frac{P(AB\mid\text{read})}{P(BB\mid\text{read})}
= \frac{p(1-p)}{(1-\varepsilon)\cdot p^2}
= \frac{1-p}{(1-\varepsilon)\cdot p} \approx \frac1p \approx 66 ,
$$

matching the table's $0.810/0.012 \approx 67$. The rare-allele prior overturns the
$2{:}1$ likelihood — it collapses the ALT-hom posterior to ~1% and hands 81% to the
heterozygote, even though the read's *likelihood* most favored $BB$. **The call is
the prior's, not the read's.** This is exactly the **depth-1 row of the motivating
table above** — 448,805 HET vs 355 ALT calls on single reads — and the overall
97% REF / 0.00% ALT-hom pattern in the SNP50K matrix
([snp50k-cohort-provenance.md](https://github.com/sawers-rellan-labs/zealtiger/blob/main/docs/snp50k-cohort-provenance.md)).

**Posterior callers inherit this, and most assume HWE.** bcftools `call -m`,
freebayes, ANGSD `-doPost`, and the imputation tools Beagle and GLIMPSE are all
**posterior callers**: each weights the genotype likelihood by a prior and reports
the genotype that maximizes the product, the **maximum-a-posteriori (MAP)** estimate.

$$
\hat G = \arg\max_G P(G\mid\text{data}) = \arg\max_G P(\text{data}\mid G)\,\pi(G) .
$$

The maximum-likelihood choice, by contrast, keeps only the likelihood
$P(\text{data}\mid G)$ and drops the prior $\pi$. The prior these callers assume is
almost always Hardy–Weinberg: at the estimated allele frequency for bcftools,
freebayes, and ANGSD `-doPost`, and at a reference panel's frequencies for Beagle
and GLIMPSE. So at a rare site every one of them turns a lone ALT read into a HET
call. The HWE prior, not the read, decides.

GATK is the exception. It does not call genotypes on the posterior: its per-sample
GT is the maximum-likelihood genotype, and the heterozygosity prior only gates
whether the site is variant. There a lone ALT read is called hom-ALT, not HET. The
companion note
[*GATK does not call genotypes on posteriors*](https://github.com/sawers-rellan-labs/zealtiger/blob/main/docs/gatk-genotype-assignment.md)
explains why, and why GATK's own heterozygote excess has a separate paralog and
coverage cause.

### Breeding prior — the single-locus NIL genotype frequencies

The NIL design is *not* HWE. Selfing after backcross drives heterozygotes to
homozygotes. Single-locus genotype frequencies (donor allele freq $0.125$; selfing
preserves it):

| design | $f_{AA}$ (REF-hom) | $f_{AB}$ (het) | $f_{BB}$ (donor-hom) | het : donor-hom \| introgressed |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| **BC2S2** (bulked skim) | 0.844 | 0.0625 | 0.0938 | 40 : 60 |
| **BC2S3** (nominal NIL) | 0.859 | 0.0312 | 0.1094 | 22 : 78 |

Note the sign — $f_{BB} > f_{AB}$ — by design, an introgressed locus is
*more often donor-homozygous than heterozygous* (78% vs 22% for BC2S3), because
selfing removes heterozygosity. (This corrects a claim in an earlier draft that the
introgression is "mostly het" — the *design* expects mostly donor-hom; the callers
*report* mostly het only because donor-hom is undetectable at $0.4\times$, §5.)

### Swapping the prior flips the single-read call

The same one alt read, now with the BC2S3 breeding prior (likelihood from §1
$\times$ prior, at $\varepsilon=0.01$, posterior normalized to sum to 1):

| genotype | likelihood $P(\text{read}\mid G)$ | $L$ | BC2S3 prior $\pi(G)$ | $\pi$ | posterior $\propto L\cdot\pi$ | $P(G\mid\text{read})$ |
|---|---|---:|---|---:|---|---:|
| $AA$ | $\varepsilon/3$ | 0.0033 | $f_{AA}$ | 0.859 | $\tfrac{\varepsilon}{3}\cdot f_{AA}$ | 0.023 |
| $AB$ | $\tfrac12\!\cdot\!\tfrac{\varepsilon}{3} + \tfrac12(1-\varepsilon)$ | 0.498 | $f_{AB}$ | 0.0312 | $\tfrac12 f_{AB}$ | 0.123 |
| $BB$ | $1-\varepsilon$ | 0.99 | $f_{BB}$ | 0.1094 | $(1-\varepsilon)\cdot f_{BB}$ | **0.855** |

Taking the ratio of the two non-REF rows' posteriors, HET now loses to ALT-hom:

$$
\frac{P(AB\mid\text{read})}{P(BB\mid\text{read})}
= \frac{\tfrac12 f_{AB}}{(1-\varepsilon)\cdot f_{BB}}
= \frac{0.5 \times 0.0312}{0.99 \times 0.1094} \approx 0.14
\quad\Rightarrow\quad \textbf{DONOR-HOM} \ (\approx 7{:}1),
$$

matching the table's $0.123/0.855$.

So the *identical read and likelihood* is called **HET under the HWE prior** and
**DONOR-HOM under the breeding prior**. This is the crux for the "fix the prior"
argument: swapping HWE → breeding frequencies is correct and worthwhile (it
counteracts the coverage-induced het-bias), **but it only relabels the default —
it adds no zygosity information.** The data still speaks at only $\approx 3$ PL. One
read cannot resolve het from hom under *any* prior; the prior decides the tie.

### The four priors side by side — and where each allele frequency comes from

We have now seen the two extremes — the HWE-at-cohort prior forces **HET**, the
BC2S3 breeding prior forces **DONOR-HOM**. Putting all four priors a real pipeline
might actually use into one comparison makes the crux explicit: **two of them are
HWE priors, and their allele frequencies have completely different origins.**

| # | prior (caller) | $\pi(AA, AB, BB)$ | allele-frequency source |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ML flat — GATK single-sample | $(\tfrac13,\tfrac13,\tfrac13)$ | none; per-sample GT $=\arg\max$ PL |
| 2 | GATK joint — `GenotypeGVCFs` | HWE at $\hat p = 0.125$ | *theoretical* BC2S3 donor freq, $f_{BB}+f_{AB}/2$ |
| 3 | bcftools — `mpileup \| call -m` | HWE at $p = 0.015$ | *empirical*, GL-based cohort mean |
| 4 | BC2S3 breeding design | $(0.859, 0.031, 0.109)$ | single-locus Mendelian NIL frequencies |

GATK sits in **two** rows: its per-sample GT is flat/ML (bar 1), but its **joint**
step (`GenotypeGVCFs`, and `CalculateGenotypePosteriors`) applies HWE at the
estimated cohort frequency (bar 2) — so joint-genotyped GATK is *not* exempt from
the het-forcing prior.

#### 2.1 The theoretical frequency $\hat p = 0.125$ (GATK joint)

Bar 2 uses the frequency **implied by the BC2S3 design itself**, not measured from
data: $\hat p = f_{BB} + f_{AB}/2 = 0.1094 + 0.0312/2 = 0.125$. This isolates the
effect of the HWE *shape*: even when a joint caller estimates the frequency
*perfectly*, HWE at $\hat p = 0.125$ gives $\pi = (0.766, 0.219, 0.016)$ — donor-hom
is $\hat p^2 = 0.016$, about $14\times$ rarer than het ($2\hat p(1-\hat p)=0.219$).
So the joint call on one read is **HET at 7:1**, despite the frequency being right.
The joint bar is the theoretical BC2S3 expectation run through HWE.

#### 2.2 The empirical frequency $p = 0.015$ — GL-based, not a hard-call count

Bar 3 uses the **measured** genome-wide mean alt-allele frequency of the real
cohort, $\bar p = 0.015$ — far below the per-locus $0.125$ because most panel sites
are near-monomorphic REF across the cohort. There are two ways to obtain such a
number, and they are not equivalent:

- **From genotype likelihoods (correct at low coverage).** Estimate $p$ directly
  from the per-sample GLs by EM (Li 2011; ANGSD `-doMaf`/`-doPost 1`; bcftools
  `call -m`), marginalizing over the unknown genotypes — no genotype is ever
  hard-called. Each sample contributes its *expected* alt dosage
  $0\cdot P(AA)+1\cdot P(AB)+2\cdot P(BB)$.
- **From called genotypes (circular).** Counting REF/ALT alleles in the called
  matrix instead re-uses calls that were themselves made under the het-forcing HWE
  prior: the prior inflates het, the het-inflated calls are counted to estimate
  $p$, and $p$ feeds the prior again.

$\bar p = 0.015$ here is the **GL-based** estimate — the posterior-caller core
(bcftools/ANGSD, Li 2011 → GLs → allele-freq prior) estimates $p$ from likelihoods
and then imposes HWE$(\bar p)$. The 448,805-vs-355 depth-1 table above is the
*consequence* of that imposed prior, not the source of $\bar p$.

> If a local pipeline instead computed $0.015$ by counting alleles in a hard-called
> VCF, that would change the reading from "correct estimator, wrong HWE shape" to
> "circular estimate *and* wrong shape." (`-doMaf` = GL-based; `bcftools query` on a
> called VCF = hard-call.)

#### 2.3 Why no single HWE frequency represents BC2S3 — and the one caller that can

BC2S3 has a **het deficit**: $f_{BB}=0.109 > f_{AB}=0.031$ (donor-hom more common
than het, because selfing collapses heterozygotes). No HWE frequency reproduces
that — HWE forces $f_{AB}=2p(1-p)\ge f_{BB}=p^2$ for all $p\le\tfrac23$. So GATK's
joint step and `CalculateGenotypePosteriors` (both HWE-locked) are *structurally*
unable to emit the BC2S3 call, whatever frequency they estimate.

The fix is an **inbreeding** term. The inbreeding-adjusted HWE prior at frequency
$p$ with coefficient $F$ is
$$
\pi = \big((1-p)^2+Fp(1-p),\;\; 2p(1-p)(1-F),\;\; p^2+Fp(1-p)\big),
$$
and at $p=0.125,\ F=0.857$ this is **exactly** $(0.859, 0.031, 0.109)$ — the BC2S3
vector ($F = 1 - f_{AB}/[2p(1-p)] = 0.857$). Selfing *is* inbreeding, so the design's
single-locus frequencies are the $F$-model frequencies, not an ad-hoc table.

Which callers can apply it? **GATK** (`GenotypeGVCFs`, `CalculateGenotypePosteriors`),
**bcftools** (`call -m`), **freebayes**, **Beagle**, and **GLIMPSE** are all
HWE-at-$p$ (i.e. $F=0$) or reference-panel priors, with no inbreeding knob — they
cannot encode $f_{BB}>f_{AB}$. **ANGSD is the exception:** with a per-individual
inbreeding coefficient from `ngsF` / `ngsF-HMM` / PCAngsd
(Vieira et al. 2013, ref. 8), its `-doPost` computes the $F$-adjusted genotype
posterior, reproducing the BC2S3 prior. (Caveat: $F$ is a genome-wide per-individual
coefficient, not a per-locus segregating-introgression prior, and $p$ is still
GL-estimated — but for the het-deficient *shape* it is exact.) So the breeding
prior is the $F$-model prior, and ANGSD `-doPost +F` is its standard implementation.

#### The four priors on one ALT read

Posterior $\propto$ likelihood $\times$ prior, normalized. Same read, same
likelihood (PL $=[25,3,0]$); only the prior changes:

| prior (caller) | $\pi(AA, AB, BB)$ | $P(AA)$ | $P(AB)$ | $P(BB)$ | call | AB : BB |
|---|---|---:|---:|---:|---|---:|
| ML flat (GATK single-sample) | $(0.333, 0.333, 0.333)$ | 0.002 | 0.333 | **0.664** | donor-hom | 0.5 |
| GATK joint, HWE $\hat p=0.125$ | $(0.766, 0.219, 0.016)$ | 0.020 | **0.858** | 0.122 | het | 7.0 |
| bcftools, HWE $p=0.015$ | $(0.970, 0.030, 0.0002)$ | 0.178 | **0.809** | 0.012 | het | 65.9 |
| BC2S3 breeding | $(0.859, 0.031, 0.109)$ | 0.023 | 0.122 | **0.855** | donor-hom | 0.1 |

**Reading.** Flat (GATK single-sample) and BC2S3 call **donor-hom** on one ALT read;
both HWE priors call **het** — GATK-joint (theoretical $\hat p=0.125$) at 7:1, bcftools
(empirical $p=0.015$) at 66:1. The call is the prior's, not the read's.

And the number of ALT reads each prior needs before it will finally call donor-hom
(with $r=0$ REF reads):

| prior (caller) | min ALT reads to $P(\text{donor-hom}) > 0.5$ |
|---|---|
| ML flat (GATK single-sample) | 1 |
| BC2S3 breeding | 1 |
| GATK joint (HWE $\hat p=0.125$) | 4 |
| bcftools (HWE $p=0.015$) | 8 |

```{r}
#| label: fig-gl-prior-lowcov
#| fig-width: 6.8
#| fig-height: 8.0
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| fig-cap: "One ALT read under four priors. **(a)** posterior $P(G\\mid\\text{one ALT read})$; the winning call is labeled above each group. **(b)** ALT reads needed before each prior crosses $P(\\text{donor-hom})>0.5$. Same read, same likelihood (PL $=[25,3,0]$) — the call is the prior's. $\\hat p=0.125$ is the theoretical BC2S3 donor frequency (GATK joint); $p=0.015$ is the empirical GL-based cohort mean (bcftools); BC2S3 $=$ inbreeding-HWE ($F=0.857$), applicable via ANGSD `-doPost +F`. Code: `scripts/fig_gl_prior_lowcov.R`."
source(here::here("scripts/fig_gl_prior_lowcov.R"))
fig
```

------

## 3. From one site to a stretch: what accumulation can and cannot do

Accumulating across a gene / haplotype / bin is the right instinct. The subtlety is
**what latent variable the accumulation is evidence *for*.** Two targets:

**(a) The genotype at each individual site.** Borrowing across sites requires an
explicit haplotype/LD model — a reference panel (Beagle/GLIMPSE, Li–Stephens) or a
founder graph (PHG). Multiplying GLs across sites of *different* genotypes is
meaningless without such a model to link them. This route needs the donor
founders / phased panel we lack, and teosinte's short-range LD undercuts it.

**(b) A single latent state shared over the stretch** — the *ancestry*
(REF / HET / donor-hom segment, or "which founder"). If the stretch has one state
$S$, each site's reads are a conditionally-independent emission and the product
concentrates the posterior on $S$:

$$
P(S \mid \text{reads}_{1:n}) \;\propto\; \pi(S)\prod_{j=1}^{n}
\underbrace{\Big[\textstyle\sum_{G} P(\text{reads}_j\mid G)\,P(G\mid S)\Big]}_{\text{emission}_j \;=\; \sum (\text{GL}) \times (\text{state-conditional genotype dist})}.
$$

This is exactly RTIGER / binhmm / PHG's `findPaths`. It works **without a panel**
because the linkage is physical contiguity plus the breeding design (few
recombinations) — not population LD.

### The count emission *is* the GL emission

For a biallelic site under an independent per-read error model, the pair
$(n_{\text{ref}}, n_{\text{alt}})$ is the **sufficient statistic** of the per-site
genotype likelihood: the emission $\sum_G P(\text{reads}_j\mid G)P(G\mid S)$ depends
on the reads only through those counts. nilHMM's count / BetaBinomial emission is a
direct parameterization of this (the BetaBinomial simply adds overdispersion for
mapping/paralog noise). Therefore:

> **"Use genotype likelihoods in the HMM instead of counts" is, for a biallelic
> site, already implemented — phrased as counts.** The caller never hard-calls per
> site (so it never inherits the bcftools 0%-ALT-hom prior artifact); it feeds soft
> per-site evidence into the along-chromosome HMM and decodes the segment.

What *full* GLs add over raw counts is real but marginal at 1 read/site: **per-base
quality weighting** (down-weight low-$Q$ bases rather than counting equally) and
mapping-quality / multiallelic handling. The information axis is the same:
$(n_{\text{ref}}, n_{\text{alt}})$ and their qualities.

------

## 4. Two different HMMs — do not conflate GATK's PairHMM

GATK's HaplotypeCaller *does* use an HMM, but it is orthogonal to the one Ruben
wants:

| | GATK **PairHMM** | **ancestry HMM** (RTIGER / nilHMM / PHG) |
|---|---|---|
| hidden path over | **bases within one read** | **marker sites along the chromosome** |
| emission | read base vs candidate haplotype (indels, base-Q) | reads at a site vs latent ancestry state |
| purpose | local re-assembly / alignment likelihood $P(\text{read}\mid\text{hap})$ | segment ancestry / genotype imputation |

GATK implements the alignment PairHMM, **not** the along-genome ancestry HMM. "They
already do this" is true only for the alignment step; it is not a reusable
component for ancestry segmentation. Keep the two distinct in the paper.

------

## 5. What survives — the information ceiling

Accumulation via the ancestry HMM buys a **confident segment state**, and the
per-marker genotype is then that state **projected/imputed onto each marker** —
PHG-style, and valid for GWAS/QTL (the mapping unit is the segment/dosage
genotype). It is *not* an independent per-site zygosity measurement.

Three limits no GL/HMM refinement removes:

1. **The signal is compressed, not hidden by hard-calling.** Outbred-donor dilution
   puts the states at alt-fractions $\approx\{0,\,d/2,\,d\}=\{0,\,0.15,\,0.30\}$
   with $d\approx0.3$ (empirically confirmed,
   [snp50k-genotype-identifiability.md](snp50k_genotype_identifiability.html) §2.5).
   GLs cannot create information the reads do not carry.
2. **Het vs donor-hom stays weak** — $0.15$ vs $0.30$ separation. A breeding prior
   ($f_{BB}>f_{AB}$) helps counteract the coverage het-bias, but does not resolve
   the zygosity.
3. **The real levers are upstream**, not in the emission: (i) **site selection** to
   donor-informative markers (raise $d$ toward 1, restoring $\{0,\tfrac12,1\}$
   separation), (ii) **fit the emission means per taxon**, (iii) use the
   **BC2S2/BC2S3 prior** in place of HWE.

**Bottom line for the "GLs will rescue it" claim:** GLs are the right soft evidence
and the ancestry HMM is the right accumulator — and both are essentially what the
count-emission caller already does. They rescue the **segment/haplotype** call (and
the imputed marker genotypes), not the isolated site, and they do not lift the
dilution ceiling.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Corrections to earlier drafts of this analysis"}
| # | superseded statement | correction |
|---|----------------------|------------|
| C1 | "GATK/samtools apply a default HWE-ish genotype prior expecting 25–50% hets." | GATK **single-sample** GT is flat-prior $\arg\max$ PL — no genotype prior. HWE enters only in the **joint** step (`GenotypeGVCFs` / `CalculateGenotypePosteriors`), as HWE at the estimated cohort $p$ (§2, §2.1). |
| C2 | Het overcalling framed as a multi-read $(\varepsilon/3)^r$ likelihood wall at 4×/12×. | The governing regime is **single-read** at 0.4× (81% of covered sites have exactly one read); the overcalling is the **rare-allele HWE prior**, not a likelihood wall (§2). |
| C3 | "RAF < 0.5 means reference over-counted." | Backwards. RAF $=$ REF/(REF+ALT); reference-mapping bias over-represents REF $\Rightarrow$ RAF $>0.5$. A clean pipeline centers RAF at 0.5. |
| C4 | "The EM under-estimates the allele frequency because of the het deficit." | It **over-estimates** ($\hat p \approx 0.139$ vs true $0.125$): the donor-hom *excess* pulls $p$ up under HWE. The frequency is ~right; the HWE *shape* is wrong (§2.3). |
| C5 | "The introgressed locus is mostly het." | BC2S3 is **het-deficient**: $f_{BB}$ (0.109) $> f_{AB}$ (0.031). By design an introgressed locus is more often donor-hom than het (§2.3). |
:::

------

## References

**Genotype-likelihood models and callers**

1. Li H (2011). A statistical framework for SNP calling, mutation discovery,
   association mapping and population genetical parameter estimation from
   sequencing data. *Bioinformatics* **27**(21):2987–2993.
   [doi:10.1093/bioinformatics/btr509](https://doi.org/10.1093/bioinformatics/btr509).
   — the samtools/bcftools GL model.
2. Korneliussen TS, Albrechtsen A, Nielsen R (2014). ANGSD: Analysis of Next
   Generation Sequencing Data. *BMC Bioinformatics* **15**:356.
   [doi:10.1186/s12859-014-0356-4](https://doi.org/10.1186/s12859-014-0356-4).
   — GL-model taxonomy + likelihood-propagation framework; software page:
   <https://www.popgen.dk/angsd/index.php/Genotype_Likelihoods>.
3. Poplin R, Ruano-Rubio V, DePristo MA, *et al.* (2017). Scaling accurate genetic
   variant discovery to tens of thousands of samples. *bioRxiv* 201178.
   [doi:10.1101/201178](https://doi.org/10.1101/201178). — GATK HaplotypeCaller.
4. DePristo MA, Banks E, Poplin R, *et al.* (2011). A framework for variation
   discovery and genotyping using next-generation DNA sequencing data.
   *Nature Genetics* **43**(5):491–498.
   [doi:10.1038/ng.806](https://doi.org/10.1038/ng.806). — the GATK framework;
   PL/GQ mechanics documented at
   <https://gatk.broadinstitute.org/hc/en-us/articles/360035890451>.

**Linkage / imputation models**

5. Li N, Stephens M (2003). Modeling linkage disequilibrium and identifying
   recombination hotspots using single-nucleotide polymorphism data.
   *Genetics* **165**(4):2213–2233.
   [doi:10.1093/genetics/165.4.2213](https://doi.org/10.1093/genetics/165.4.2213).
   — the haplotype-mosaic (Li–Stephens) model behind panel imputation (§3a).
6. Jensen SE, Charles JR, Muleta K, *et al.* (2020). A sorghum practical haplotype
   graph facilitates genome-wide imputation and cost-effective genomic prediction.
   *The Plant Genome* **13**(1):e20009.
   [doi:10.1002/tpg2.20009](https://doi.org/10.1002/tpg2.20009).
   — the founder-graph (PHG) route (§3, §5).
7. Hui R, D’Atanasio E, Cassidy LM, Scheib CL, Kivisild T (2020). Evaluating
   genotype imputation pipeline for ultra-low coverage ancient genomes.
   *Scientific Reports* **10**:18542.
   [doi:10.1038/s41598-020-75387-w](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-020-75387-w).
   — reference-panel imputation at ultra-low coverage (§3a).

**Inbreeding-aware genotype calling**

8. Vieira FG, Fumagalli M, Albrechtsen A, Nielsen R (2013). Estimating inbreeding
   coefficients from NGS data: impact on genotype calling and allele frequency
   estimation. *Genome Research* **23**(11):1852–1861.
   [doi:10.1101/gr.157388.113](https://doi.org/10.1101/gr.157388.113).
   — the per-individual $F$-adjusted genotype posterior (ANGSD `ngsF`, §2.3).
